title: Kestrel Scale Search Solutions
preview:
Account summary: Kestrel is a B2B search platform used by several Fortune 500 clients. Moving from a custom ES+FAISS stack to an opinionated vendor to reduce ops burden. Primary drivers: unit cost per query at scale, predictable tail latency for reranking flows, and compliance/residency needs.

Key technical posture and asks: they plan to index 400M-600M dense vectors initially, target 1B+ within 18 months. Workloads are heavy on embeddings + reranking: candidate recall from embeddings then 3-stage reranker cascade (cheap model for coarse rerank + mid-tier reranker + final expensive ensemble). Want autoscaling for spikes (daily peak windows), persistent KV cache for long sessions, and deterministic batching controls to hit cost SLOs.

Quotes / SDR notes: "We need <150ms P90 for first-pass retrieval and <900ms end-to-end rerank for 99% of queries" — Head of Search.
"If Redwood can show 40-60% per-query cost improvement vs our tuned FAISS+GPU fleet, procurement will move fast" — VP Eng.

Security & procurement: heavy emphasis on VPC/private control plane, BYOK KMS/HSM support, audit logs retained for 3 years, and EU data residency for a subset of customers. Legal asked for carrier-grade SLA language + right-to-audit clause.

POC ask: run a 2-week scale benchmark (ingest ~50M vectors/day) with 2 configurations: Dedicated (reserved GPUs with batching profiles) and Private (on-prem compute). Provide tokenized cost model showing cost/query across QPS ranges (10qps -> 2k qps). Include quantized model profiles (int8 / int4) and expected quality drop per profile.

Notes on current stack & migration: currently using hybrid CPU+GPU FAISS with bespoke sharding. They have internal embeddings pipeline (TensorFlow) that produces 1536-d vectors; open to switching to smaller dims if cost shows benefit. Team will run Redwood Optimize recommendations and wants per-route profiling and tracing integrated into their observability (Datadog).

title: Pyxis Orbit Embeds
preview:
Summary: Mid-market SaaS (product analytics) evaluating Redwood Hosted API first for rapid POC then a move to Dedicated for stable throughput. Primary workload = embeddings + semantic search across telemetry, product docs, and customer notes. 

Customer pains/requirements (verbatim & paraphrase):
- "We need nightly batch ingest of 200k new vectors and realtime updates for ~2k hot docs/day."
- Must keep vector payloads in regional S3 and ensure embeddings never leave customer VPC for Dedicated option.
- Latency target: search+rerank (3-5 top hits) end-to-end <= 120ms for interactive UI, background report jobs can tolerate higher latency.
- Throughput target: 2k qps during peak product telemetry queries; realistic steady-state ~150 qps. Batch ingest windows ~2-4 hours nightly.
- Cost sensitivity: looking to reduce per-vector cost vs current OpenAI spend; interested in quantization and batching recommendations.

Technical notes:
- Current stack: ingest pipeline -> metadata store (Postgres) + Pinecone for vector index; plan to port to Redwood's embeddings endpoint and use Redwood caching for hot keys.
- POC plan: 50k doc sample run (completed), measure host API latency & token cost, test vector DB sync latency (Redwood -> Pinecone via connector), evaluate Dedicated throughput for batch window.
- Integration asks: built-in connector to Pinecone (or native Redwood-backed index), support for batched embed creation with idempotency, doc-level metadata preservation, and dedupe logic.
- Security asks: SOC2 attestation required, DPA negotiation (KMS + key expiry language), SSO via SAML for admin console, audit logs retention 1 year, and optional EU data residency for EU customers.

POC observations so far:
- Hosted API embed latency in our light test ~15-25ms per embed (good).
- Bulk batch ingestion of 50k docs completed but observed throttle jitter at 5k/min; SE suggests Dedicated burst capacity for nightly windows.
- Relevancy: basic semantic search results acceptable but Pyxis wants tuned reranker + prompt templates to improve click-through.

Commercials & interest:
- Short-term: continue Hosted API POC while Redwood provides a Dedicated sizing estimate for a 3-month committed pool.
- Pricing sensitivity: wants a blended cost-per-embed under current OpenAI spend; interested in dedicated commit for predictable monthly bill.

Action items:
- AE (Jordan) to send Dedicated sizing worksheet + example pricing overlay (due 2026-03-10).
- SE (Maya) to produce connector doc for Pinecone sync and checklist for idempotent batch ingestion (due 2026-03-12).
- Engineering to answer VPC peering options and KMS integrations (internal RFC requested).

Stated quotes from stakeholders: "If Dedicated gives us consistent throughput for nightly jobs and better unit economics, we'll push procurement through in April."

References: Fireflies ff_2026-02-18_84a7c1, deck in Drive, pricing spreadsheet stub in Drive.

title: Nimbus Knowledge Labs
preview:
Summary: Nimbus Knowledge Labs is a rapidly growing SaaS helpdesk platform (B2B) that wants to replace current keyword/full-text search with a production RAG pipeline: vector embeddings + lightweight reranker for top-k answer precision. They are specifically focused on high QPS (peak ~1k-1.5k qps), moderate index corpus (1–3M canonical docs, ~40–70GB embeddings), and tight median latency targets (median end-to-end <200ms for interactive agent lookups).

Workload details / requirements: 
- Ingest cadence: nightly delta + real-time small doc adds (~500/day).
- Index scale target: initial 1M docs, plan to scale to 3M within 12 months. Estimated embedding storage ~50GB (float16) before compression/quantization.
- Query profile: 70% short agent queries (1–3 sentences), 30% long contextual retrievals. Expected P95 latency budget for retrieval+rerank: <350ms; median <200ms.
- Throughput: concurrent QPS spikes up to 1.2k, sustained 600–800 qps during business hours.
- Architecture preference: retrieval (ANN) + cross-encoder reranker for top-10, then lightweight answer synthesis. Reranker model must be small/optimized for latency (e.g., distilled cross-encoder or 2-stage hybrid).
- Cost sensitivity: target cost per end-user query (including retrieval + rerank + generation overhead) under $0.005 if possible; very sensitive to token-gen costs and rerank compute. Will trade model size for latency/price where accuracy delta is small.
- Model preferences: open/quantizable models preferred (Llama variants or Redwood-verified variants). Will evaluate Redwood-hosted model variants and capacity tiers. Need clear perf/cost profile for each model.

Security & compliance: 
- Requires SOC2; SSO via SAML mandatory.
- EU data residency for EU customer datasets (some customers require EU-only storage).
- Need audit logs for queries and document access for 90 days. KMS integration for runtime keys is desirable.

Operational concerns: 
- High QPS means batching + KV caching important; customer asked if Redwood runtime supports continuous batching and prefix caching to reduce per-token cost.
- Concern about reranker adding high CPU/GPU overhead — wants recommended config for cross-encoder batching and quantized models.
- Wants traffic shaping / fallback: can Redwood route to cheaper model variants under high load (automatic fallback)? POC should include failover scenarios.

POC ask / success criteria: 
1) Load test: ingest 1M docs (customer-provided sample), run synthetic QPS profile up to 1.2k, capture latency P50/P95, and end-to-end cost per 10k queries.
2) Quality: reranker+retriever Precision@3 improvement vs baseline BM25 (customer will provide gold set ~600 queries).
3) Security: demonstrate SAML SSO flow + storage in EU region for EU-labeled docs.
4) Ops: show per-route dashboards, token-level cost breakdown, and suggested optimize changes (batching/quantization) with estimated savings.

Quotes / call snippets: 
- "If we can't hit median <200ms at 1k qps we won't be able to replace the current fast keyword fallback." — Head of Search (Alex R.).
- "We're not married to any vector DB; our main requirement is predictable cost and SLA." — CTO (Priya S.)

Next steps (short):
- Send POC SOW + benchmark plan (AE).
- Provide Redwood perf/cost matrix for candidate models (SE).
- Legal to follow up on data residency clause (Cust. legal).

Attached artifacts: pricing SOW draft in Drive, security questionnaire submitted (drive link), Fireflies recording of 2026-02-28 QPS discussion.

title: Keystone Ops Assurance
preview:
Account snapshot: global market-data provider; latency-sensitive trading assist and real-time enrichment. Key asks centered on ironclad SLA (uptime + credits), deterministic latency, reserved GPU planning and predictable maintenance windows.

SLA negotiation highlights:
- Customer requests: 99.99% uptime for primary trading endpoints, 99.95% for non-critical APIs. Wants credits to scale and not be de minimis.
- Redwood baseline: proposed 99.95% platform SLA for Dedicated; offered add-on for 99.99% with increased reserved capacity and dedicated engineer.
- Credit schedule discussed (customer pushed for formula). Current proposed sliding scale (example):
  * uptime >= 99.95%: 0% credits
  * 99.9% <= uptime < 99.95%: 10% of monthly Dedicated fee
  * 99.0% <= uptime < 99.9%: 30% credit
  * uptime < 99.0%: 100% credit (customer insisted on cap at 100% monthly fee)
- Exclusions: scheduled maintenance, force majeure. Customer insists scheduled maintenance capped to 4 hours/month for trading endpoints and must have 72-hour advance notice.
- Maintenance windows: customer prefers 02:00-05:00 ET; Redwood recommended regional maintenance but agreed to white-list trading endpoints for patched lanes.

Latency & throughput SLOs:
- Latency targets for chat/trading assist: p95 < 120ms, p99 < 300ms on configured Dedicated cluster under expected concurrent sessions (10k active sessions).
- Throughput guarantees: baseline sustained throughput per cluster 8k tokens/sec (A100-equivalent); Keystone requests aggregate sustained 64k tokens/sec for production; Redwood proposed reserved pool of 8x A100-80GB nodes (baseline) + 2 hot-spare nodes, autoscale to 12 nodes for burst (30-min spin-up SLA).
- Fallbacks: automatic routing to lower-cost variants acceptable for non-critical queries, but trading endpoints must not fall back without explicit failover policy and customer consent.

Operational support / escalation:
- Support SLA: P1 (system down impacting trading) response < 15 minutes, P1 remediation updates every 30 minutes, 24/7 on-call; P2 < 60 minutes; P3 < 4 hours. Customer requests named on-call engineer for first 90 days post-go-live.
- Diagnostics: access to token-level latency traces, request logs (scrubbed PII), and billing breakdowns required for monthly review.

Security & compliance notes:
- CISO quote: "cannot accept key escrow — all keys must be customer-managed KMS; proof of key retention and rotation policy."
- Data residency: primary production in us-east-1, backups replicated to us-west-2; no eu data allowed for trading ML.

Commercials and GPU planning:
- Pricing sensitivity: willing to commit to 12-24 month reserved contract if SLA and maintenance windows acceptable. Estimated ARR band discussed $500k-$1.5M.
- GPU plan: initial reserved 8x A100 (80GB) per cluster; add 2 hot spares; option to burst to hosted pool (pay-as-you-go) for unpredictable spikes. Customer asked for clear lead time (4 weeks) for adding physical capacity in Dedicated.

Open legal redlines / blockers:
- Legal wants explicit language for scheduled maintenance caps and exclusion list; remove vague "reasonable efforts" language around credits.
- Need to clarify credit calculation baseline (is it regional availability zone or global cluster availability?).

Next steps (short):
- Redwood to provide SLA markups with explicit maintenance caps and sample credit clause by 2026-03-17.
- SE to deliver final GPU sizing quote and lead times; include hot-spare cost and burst rates.
- Legal to arrange redline session w/ Keystone legal (week of 2026-03-22).

Stubs / references: pricing deck and draft SLA in Drive (see linked_drive_docs). Fireflies IDs for conversation capture. Jira INFRA-1129 opened to capture provisioning lead-time question.

title: PulseCode Labs
preview:
Account brief: PulseCode Labs is building an IDE-first code assistant plugin for JetBrains (IntelliJ/PyCharm) that uses a hybrid local-index + remote model approach. Primary ask: fast, token-efficient completions with offline fallback and strict telemetry controls.

- 2026-02-20 architecture deep-dive (Maya/Evan) — discussed runtime options: lightweight local server vs in-plugin native bridge, concerns about Apple silicon binaries.

Technical summary / requirements:
- Plugin target: IntelliJ 2023.3+ and PyCharm Professional. Must run a local indexer that builds inverted indexes and symbol graphs every CI run and incrementally on file save.
- Completion path: try local neural cache (on-device quantized model) for sub-50ms completions; fallback to Redwood hosted model variant when local miss or for expensive generative edits.
- Streaming: need low-latency chunked streaming to the editor (first token under 30ms preferred in intra-datacenter), with keystroke-level cancellation semantics.
- Model management: pin model versions per workspace, provide rollback, A/B testing for model variants across teams.
- Privacy: telemetry opt-out by default; plugin must support running entirely air-gapped for enterprise customers.
- Ops: want per-route cost reporting and token-level visibility in Console; recommendations from Optimize for batching/caching are important to reduce infra spend.

POC scope agreed:
- 4-week POC: local indexer + IntelliJ plugin prototype + Redwood private endpoint for heavier inference.
- Success metrics: completion latency median <45ms for local hits, fallback success rate >95%, token cost under target.

Quotes / coms:
- Amit (CTO): "If we can't guarantee snappy completions offline, dev adoption will stall."
- Engineering lead (Ruth): "We need clear controls: can we pin a smaller quantized model for interns vs senior engs?"

Sales/logistics:
- Pricing discussion in drive doc. Roadblock: legal wants a standard DPA with clear data flow diagrams. InfoSec opened SEC-1109.

- Redwood to provide macOS M1/M2 deployment notes and prebuilt binary checklist.
- SE (Maya) to send plugin manifest and sample IntelliJ extension skeleton by 2026-03-12.
- Schedule POC kickoff; coordinate with PulseCode infra for VPC peering or on-prem agent install.

title: NimbusForge Product Labs
preview:
Quick summary:
- Mid-market B2B SaaS (product analytics for mid-size merchants). Product team evaluating Hosted API first (trial started 2026-02-18), then moving to Dedicated if POC metrics hit targets.
- Primary goal: deploy a customer-facing assistant + internal doc search; also want embeddings for similarity + reranking on search results.
POC / Trial findings (hosted API):
- Trial window: 2 weeks (2026-02-18 to 2026-03-03).
- Instrumentation collected: token counts, per-request latency, QPS, payload size, error rates.
- Observed steady-state QPS during business hours: 8-12 QPS. Peak bursts measured: single-hour peaks up to 48 QPS (marketing campaign).
- Avg prompt tokens: ~85. Avg completion tokens: ~300 (chat/generation flows). Embedding calls: small but heavy by volume; avg embed size ~768 dims.
- Token mix (by requests): 60% chat/generation, 30% embeddings, 10% reranking. Token mix (by tokens): ~72% generation tokens, 20% prompt, 8% embedding tokens.
- Latency targets: product team wants p95 < 300ms for chat generation (accept p99 <= 700ms). Host API measured p95 ~320ms on hosted trial (acceptable with a note).
- Cost sensitivity: target TCO such that hosted costs < $0.02 per active MAU for their support assistant product; looking for dedicated to reduce per-token cost ~30-50%.
Dedicated sizing ask / forecast needs:
- Ask from SE: guidance on Dedicated GPU pool sizing for production traffic:
  - Sustained baseline target: 20 QPS (conservative steady-state across regions)
  - Burst planning: handle 60 QPS spikes for up to 15 minutes (campaign traffic)
  - Concurrency target: 40-80 concurrent inference sessions (chat turns + streaming)
  - Token throughput: estimate 800M tokens/month steady-state, spiking to 1.2B during quarter-end promotions.
  - Model preferences: prefer optimized Llama-3 style variant (quantized + KV cache enabled), fallback to smaller Llama-2-like family for red-lines.
- Requested metrics from Redwood: per-model QPS per GPU at target 300-token completion, expected p95/p99 latencies per GPU config, memory/kv cache footprint, recommended autoscale/burst headroom.
POC plan & milestones:
- 2026-02-18: Hosted trial started (Jordan setup, Riley guided test prompts).
- 2026-02-25: Mid-trial sync — product demo of assistant flows; recorded Fireflies ff_8342_20260223.
- 2026-03-03: Trial close — Riley delivered trial metrics spreadsheet (drive:/pocs/nf-llm-hosted-trial-results-2026.xlsx).
- 2026-03-09: Redwood to provide Dedicated sizing doc & price bands.
- 2026-03-16: Internal security review of Redwood SSO/KMS checklist scheduled.
Procurement / Commercial notes:
- They prefer a 12–24 month committed Dedicated contract if unit economics justify it.
- Will consider a 6-month Dedicated pilot with overage bands to de-risk.
- Ask: transparent token pricing + predictable monthly committed QPS pricing.
Security & compliance:
- Must support SSO (SAML) and bring-your-own-KMS for key rotation.
- Audit logs retention requirement: 12 months for inference metadata (they will push for longer for enterprise customers).
Open questions / blockers (detail):
- Need Redwood confirmation on EU data residency for embeddings storage (legal hold).
- How much caching/kv persistence can be configured per-customer to reduce dedicated capacity?
- Exact GPU instance class recommendation: customer wants an explicit mapping: X GPUs = Y sustained QPS at 300-token avg.
Sales/next steps (short):
- Jordan to get Redwood sizing doc from runtime team by 2026-03-09.
- Riley to run internal cost model comparing Hosted vs Dedicated (sheet attached).
- Legal to review draft SOW & privacy addendum once sizing/pricing locked.
Misc notes (voice snippets):
- Quote from Head of Product: "If we can hit p95 <300ms at predictable cost, Dedicated is a no-brainer."
- CTO concern: "We need precise residency & KMS behavior before multi-region rollout."
- Typical CRM shorthand present in call notes: "AE/JB intro; SE walkthrough; POC data sets uploaded to staging; waiting on infra numbers."

title: MicroVault Payments
preview:
Inbound path: self-serve signup 2026-02-18 (trial credits used). AE outreach 2026-02-20 (Jordan Blake).

Recent timeline (bullets):
- 2026-02-18: sign-up via marketing landing page, created trial org, tried embeddings endpoint with sample transaction logs.
- 2026-02-20: AE intro call, capture requirements and security checklist.
- 2026-03-01: Technical call with CTO + Head of Eng (FF-20260301-7890). Demo of hosted API requested.
- 2026-03-04: SE follow-up (Priya) sent SSO/SAML checklist + quickstart repo link.
- Planned: 2-week POC starting after SSO doc review; aim to validate embeddings recall and chat latency.

Use-case & requirements summary:
- Workloads: customer support chat (internal CS agents), transaction classification for routing/fraud triage, embedding-based doc + log search, KYC doc parsing (light NLP).
- Latency targets: p50 chat < 200ms (goal), p95 < 400ms acceptable during POC.
- Throughput: low to medium (initially ~5-20 QPS burst for chat), embeddings batch jobs nightly.
- Cost sensitivity: high — startup budget, looking to optimize tokens and use quantized open models where possible.
- Models: prefer vendor to certify small/quantized open models; open to Redwood-verified defaults. Fallback routing to cheaper quant variants for non-critical traffic.
- Routing/fallback: want simple policy to route heavy batch embeddings to cheaper region; keep chat on low-latency tier.

Security/compliance notes:
- Primary ask: vendor SOC2 evidence early in eval. CTO: "we cannot move forward without SOC2 summary and data handling SOPs".
- SSO: SAML required for production; want sample metadata and IdP integration steps.
- Data residency: EU customers possible; need clarity on storage and retention controls (currently hosted in US).
- KMS: interested in customer-managed keys in future (VPC/private deployment discussion for Q3 if POC OK).

POC plan (proposed):
- Duration: 2 weeks.
- Scope: embed sample payment logs into embeddings index; integrate chat for CS agents using hosted API; transaction classifier endpoint.
- Success metrics: embeddings recall@5 > 0.85, chat p50 latency <200ms, cost per 1k tokens within estimate.

Quotes / verbatim snippets:
- CTO: "Need SOC2 in vendor profile, otherwise procurement stops us."
- Head of Eng: "Prefer SSO + SAML in prod; can demo with API keys while we test."
- Product lead: "If POC latency/cost looks good, we'll expand to more flows Q2."

Next steps (short):
- Jordan: send pricing snapshot + sample SOC2 extract (done 2026-03-05).
- Priya (SE): share SSO quickstart repo, sample SAML metadata, and a minimal code example for embeddings + chat.
- Schedule 30m dev demo with Head of Eng and 2 devs week of 2026-03-08.

Open/blocked: SOC2 evidence, SSO metadata exchange, PO approval timeline (CFO), confirm EU data residency approach.

Linked artifacts: drive:/decks/microvault-pricing-proposal-v1, drive:/security/soc2-summary.pdf. Jira ticket INFRA-1342 created for SSO onboarding questions.

Notes style: shorthand + action items — typical SMB evaluation.

title: Maple Rail Dynamics
preview:
CRM notes — mostly fragmenty, action-focused.
- Account: mid-market B2B SaaS (product analytics for retail logistics).
- Primary contacts: Amira Novak (CTO), Henrik Lau (Infra Lead), Priya Kapoor (Product Lead).
- Use-case: in-app assistant + contextual doc search + reranking for recommendations. Expected 40k monthly active users; peak bursts during morning/close-of-day.
- Initial preference: start with Hosted API to speed time-to-value, run 3-week POC against production-ish traffic via a staging token. If POC meets latency + cost targets, transition to Dedicated reserved capacity with EU-only residency and VPC peering.
- EU residency: "need all inference and KV/cache stored in EU, logs accessible for 90d, keys in our KMS" — CTO quote during 2/11. Legal pre-review flagged EU-only SLA language and auditability for logs.
- Latency requirement: Product lead asked for P95 <120ms for short chat flows (<=256 tokens); acceptable tradeoff if read-only reranking can be routed to lower-cost zone. Henrik: "we can tolerate 200-250ms for embeddings but not for live chat."
- Multi-region routing: evaluating fallback strategy (EU primary -> NA secondary) vs. active-active. Concern: cache affinity and KV cache warming will affect cold-start latency; want recommendations in Optimize report.
- Pricing sensitivity: mid-market budget. Interested in committed throughput discount for Dedicated; requested two pricing options: (A) Hosted API with regional tenancy + higher per-token for EU-only, (B) Dedicated 3x-GPU pool with burst and reserved baseline.
- POC success metrics: endpoint P95 <160ms on hosted test traffic with 95% pass, error rate <0.5%, cost per 1k tokens < $0.70 (benchmark).
- Support requests: SE to provide runbook for VPC peering and egress cost estimate; Security asked for an SOC2/ISO export pack and KMS integration diagram.
- Quotes: "If we can't get EU residency in-hosted, we need Dedicated or a Private deployment" (Amira).
- Next steps: Lila to deliver Dedicated sizing + EU region pricing by 2026-03-09; Jordan to schedule legal drop-in with Maple legal and Redwood security.

title: Harborlight CX Sentinel
preview:
POC scope: agent-assist + ticket summarization + tool-calling integration with ServiceNow. Requirements summary:
- RAG with multi-source retrieval: Zendesk articles, Confluence, S3 attachments, older ticket corpus.
- Strong citation requirement: agents must see source links and confidence score.
- Freshness: nightly snapshot + on-demand reindex for edited articles; legal wants 24h SLA for new content to be searchable.
- Reliability: need predictable latency during spikes (Black Friday-like loads). Interested in dedicated pools or private deployment for latency guarantees.
- Cost sensitivity: large seasonal spikes — wants autoscaling with predictable billing and a fallback cheaper model for low-priority queries.
- Tool-calling: must be able to open/append ServiceNow tickets and suggest macros; audit trail required.
- Security: SSO/SAML, SOC2 attestation, KMS/HSM for key custody, per-region data residency (US + EMEA).
Recent call quotes: "We need the answers to be provably sourced — agents can't guess." — VP of Support.
POC success criteria: citation recall > 92%, summary compression ratio avg 6x, 95th-pct latency < 450ms under target concurrency, no data leakage across accounts.
Staging notes: we shipped a small snapshot to the dedicated POC cluster; SE is tuning prefix caching and batch window to smooth spikes.
Internal next actions: draft SOW (pricing for reserved burst capacity), prepare SOC2 artifacts, map storage buckets to Redwood private connectors.
CRM shorthand: needs legal loop-in for KMS clause; procurement will require 3 bids (Redwood vs. two infra vendors).

title: Arcadia Promptcraft
preview:
Overview: Arcadia Promptcraft builds a VS Code extension that provides streaming completions, inline suggestions and fuzzy signature help for JS/TS + Python. Team is engineering-first; prototype shipped but performance issues at scale.

Primary ask: low-latency streaming + per-extension per-user rate limits. They want median inline suggestion RTT <80ms for local dev workflows and tail P99 <200ms for short completions (<64 tokens). Want to support model/version pinning per-channel so customers can choose a lightweight quantized variant for IDE while defaulting to higher-quality model on demand.

Security: Must integrate with customer SSO (SAML), audit logs for suggestion events, and KMS integration for secrets. Data residency not strict but they want VPC option for enterprise customers.

Metrics/POC success criteria: 1) median suggestion latency <80ms; 2) acceptance rate uplift >25% vs baseline; 3) cost per active dev under target ($X/day, discussed in pricing deck); 4) no regressions in suggestion quality measured by curated eval prompts.

Quote from CTO on call: "If we can't guarantee smooth inline experience at scale, customers churn — latency is everything."

Notes from demo: stream looks good on 5-10 dev scale; on 500+ DAU they saw buffering and token drops. Discussed batching/caching and per-session KV cache to keep prefix latencies low. SE presented alternate: route short inline completions to a quantized code model with aggressive KV caching, fallback to larger model for multi-line completions.

Operational asks: per-extension quotas, dynamic rate limits by org tier, token-level cost telemetry in console, hooks to disable streaming for specific users/orgs.

Proposed next steps: get security packet completed, share POC test harness (drive link), align on SLOs and rate-limit config, sign POC SOW (6 weeks).

title: HexaPatch Labs
preview:
Account background: HexaPatch Labs builds an IDE-integrated assistant focused on GitHub-style PR review workflows. Small engineering org (approx 120 EE) focused on developer velocity for mid-market SaaS. They ship a VS Code extension + server side review service.

Key asks / requirements:
- Ultra-low token latency for streaming diff summaries and inline comment generation (goal: <150ms token latency for short responses, <300ms end-to-end for a 6-10 line diff).
- Support for model/version pinning per-customer and per-workspace; rollback on bad generation.
- Deterministic reranking API for suggestions (stable scores across runs).
- Efficient KV/prefix caching so repeated file-context compiles cheaply (hot files across teams).
- Cost-sensitivity: wants batching and cache-driven downsampling to control token spend for heavy PR traffic.
- Security: must support SSO (SAML), audit logs, KMS integration, and EU data residency for some customers.

Recent timeline / activity (CRM style):
- 2025-10-03: initial inbound (trial sign-up) - flagged by Growth for high usage pattern.
- 2025-11-12: AE intro call (Jordan) - discussed product fit and rough ARR. Action: send product one-pager. (drive link provided).
- 2026-01-14: technical deep-dive with SE (Maya) - went through streaming architecture and model pin requirements. SE notes: interested in dedicated hosts for predictable latency.
- 2026-02-18: security review kickoff with Redwood security; Fireflies: ff_2026-02-18_hexapatch_01. Asked about KMS/hardware isolation and audit log retention.
- 2026-03-01: product demo + POC scope agreed: 2-week POC to validate latency/throughput on sample PR corpus (10k diffs). Fireflies: ff_2026-03-01_arch-review. Resourcing: they requested dedicated SE hours for benchmark tuning.
- POC milestone plan: week 0: ingest PR corpus + baseline metrics; week 1: run streaming + reranking test matrix; week 2: iterate quant/ batching params and collect cost report.

Technical notes from calls (shorthand):
- "Need streaming that starts token-forwarding asap — extension UX cannot wait for full decode."
- Preferred models: open weight family first (cost) but require Redwood validation on quantized 13B / 7B variants. Want guidance on kernel selection and batch sizing.
- Pinning: per-workspace pin + canary rollout to subset of orgs. Must expose rollout controls in Console.
- Reranking: deterministic reranker desired; they will supply labeled data for tuning.
- Throughput/scale: target 200 concurrent active sessions during peak; average PR summary request size ~120 tokens.

Commercial / procurement notes:
- Interested in a hybrid approach: Hosted API for dev ramp, move to Dedicated once >5k seats.
- Budget: initial POC funded from R&D; procurement decision depends on POC results (targeting Q2 enterprise procurement).

Open questions / next actions:
- SE to provide benchmark template and required telemetry hooks for Console (latency per-token, batching, cache hit rate).
- Security to provide KMS integration diagram + sample SLA language for data residency.
- Schedule VPC peering call (requesting availability wk of 2026-03-10).
- Legal: draft minimal Data Processing Addendum for EU customers.

Stitchy CRM fragments / quotes:
- "We cannot have the extension hang — streaming must be immediate." — Head of Product.
- "If we can hit 150ms token latency we can replace our current in-house model routing." — CTO.

Linked artifacts: pricing deck (drive), POC test corpus (drive), security FAQ (drive).

Short-term ask from AE: 2 SE days for POC setup, dedicated trial capacity (prefer reserved SLA), and security packet for procurement.

Prognosis: warm; high conversion probability if POC validates latency and security items.

title: Deepwell Media Indexing
preview:
Summary:
- Deepwell runs a 20M-document media archive (news articles, transcripts, image captions). avg doc length ~5k words; many very long transcripts.
- Primary need: fast, high-quality search for editors and legal teams (latency target: <300ms to first relevant for UX; 95th <800ms including reranker).
- Cost constraint: want cost/query ~ <$0.02 overall for typical sessions (initial target $0.008 - $0.015 per query), high sensitivity to per-token + reranker GPU time.

Technical / POC plan (from AE/SE call):
- Retrieval stack: dense bi-encoder for candidate retrieval (FAISS) + GPU cross-encoder reranker for top-K. K in discussion (50-200).
- Indexing scale: expect ~80M chunks after chunking (current estimate). Concerned about index sharding, RAM footprint, and search tail latency.
- Chunking experiments: compare 512-token chunks vs 1024-token chunks + 20% overlap. Tradeoffs discussed: larger chunks reduce number of vectors (cost) but increase noisy context for embedding; smaller chunks increase recall for long queries but increases storage and retrieval cost.
- Embeddings: prefer an open embedding model with 1536-d vectors. SE suggested test with sentence-transformers/all-mpnet-base-v2 and a newer open 1536-d candidate in Redwood catalog.
- Reranker tuning: start with a distilled cross-encoder (DistilBERT cross-encoder) for latency; evaluate accuracy uplift vs a stronger BERT-large cross-encoder. Discussed using model temperature/soft-label distillation and calibrating score thresholds to reduce reranker calls (only rerank top-40 most likely).
- Caching & batching: plan to test prefix and query-level caching to reduce calls for repeat queries; dynamic batching for reranker to amortize GPU time.

Security/compliance notes:
- Must be VPC-hosted; legal requires audit logs + KMS integration. Data residency: US-only for now. Security team will run Redwood security questionnaire next week.

Interactions & timeline:
- 2026-01-10: Intro call (AE+SE) — collected high-level requirements.
- 2026-01-28: Deep technical working session; shared sample dataset (anonymized).
- 2026-02-12: POC kickoff call (Fireflies FF-2026-02-12-4582). Agreed on 3-phase POC: ingest + chunk test -> retrieval+rerank -> perf/cost tuning.
- 2026-02-26: Security scoping call (Fireflies FF-2026-02-26-4729).
- 2026-03-01: Engineering sync; Redwood provided cost model (see drive doc).

Quotes / call snippets:
- "If we can't get first-relevant under 300ms the editors won't adopt it" — Head of Search.
- "We need a clear cost per query at 1M queries/month scale" — VP Product.

Open action items / POC success criteria:
- Demonstrate indexing cadence: can ingest 5M docs/day and keep vector index updated with <2hr lag.
- Latency: median to first relevant <300ms, 95th <800ms in Dedicated environment.
- Accuracy: reranker increases eventual MAP@10 by >8% over bi-encoder only baseline.
- Cost: target average infra cost per search session <$0.02 (including retrieval + rerank).

Risks:
- Index growth (80M chunks) increases storage and retrieval costs above estimate. Need to test chunk consolidation heuristics (document-level anchors, metadata priors).
- Reranker GPU cost could dominate if top-K is large; SE suggests top-40 gating + score-thresholding.

Next steps (short):
- Schedule Dedicated canary POC week of 2026-03-15. SE to prepare ingestion script + sample index.
- Legal to push back SSO/SAML details; AE to provide encrypted SAML metadata.
- Redwood to run initial chunk-size A/B on 200k docs and share cost/latency matrix.

CRM style notes (short/fragments):
- Decision maker: VP Product (Samira Hale).
- Procurement lead: Finance (RFP pending).
- Wants reference from similar publisher — ask BD for reference intro.
- Healthy urgency: pilot by end of Q2 2026.

title: HaloQueue Automation
preview:
POC focused on automated ticket and email summarization + auto-tagging for Zendesk and Freshdesk workflows. Primary ask: generate 2–3 line TL;DR summaries suitable for agents and macros, and deterministic tag suggestions (intent, product area, SLA tier).

Initial discovery: product team wants to reduce handle time for Tier 1 by 30% and improve triage accuracy for routing to specialists. Current volume: 25k tickets/month, peak bursts 2–3x during promotions. They run Zendesk with custom macros; Freshdesk on a few pilot lines. Data retention policy: 2 years, some EU-origin data must stay in EU region.

Technical constraints: must integrate with Zendesk app framework (app needs to pass OAuth and run within agent UI), support webhook-based async summarization for long threads, and offer deterministic tag outputs to feed into existing triggers/macros. Important: fallback behavior during spikes (queue to LLM Lite model or cached summary generation) and transparent token/cost metrics for finance.

Quote from SVP Product: "If summaries can't be produced in < 600ms for single-turn messages, agents won't adopt it — but batch post-processing for end-of-day summaries is fine." Wants audit trail for every generated summary.

Security: vendor must be SOC2 compliant; prefer VPC/private control plane for sensitive pilots. Asked about KMS support and customer-managed keys. Also requested data residency guarantees for EU customer subsets.

POC scope: 1) real-time in-agent summary (Zendesk sidebar), 2) nightly batch summaries for backlog triage, 3) auto-tagging rules exported as CSV/JSON to import into macros, 4) failure mode: fallback to shorter summary + link to raw thread.

Commercial sensitivity: price-conscious but open to committed capacity if latency/SLA proven. Procurement wants 12-month committed price and an option to expand during Q4 sale spikes.

Stubs/links: Fireflies meetings above, security questionnaire started (SEC-1292), POC dataset shared in drive folder, demo recording uploaded to drive deck.

title: Oakridge Axon Platforms
preview:
Initial contact: 2025-11-05 (inbound via webinar).
Prospect: mid-market B2B SaaS (productivity suite + in-app assistant).
Early asks: predictable latency, lower token unit-cost for heavy chat, embeddings for search, reranking for recommendations.
Timeline (short):
- 2025-11-18: Hosted API quickstart + sandbox access (AE demo).
- 2025-12-02: SE architecture review — focused on VPC peering, SSO flow, data residency.
- 2026-01-14: Product demo (Fireflies ff_2026-01-14_oa01) — liked streaming + function-calling.
- 2026-02-20: Pricing + Dedicated capabilities deep-dive (Fireflies ff_2026-02-20_demo02).
Decision point: start with Hosted API proofs, then move to Dedicated because of steady high QPS and need for isolation.
POC ask (agreed): 2-week Dedicated POC to validate latency, tail behavior, and cost gains. SE will run canary traffic pattern + rollback test.
POC Plan (high-level):
- Canary split: 10% production-mirrored traffic -> Dedicated canary pool, 90% stays on Hosted during canary phase.
- Model variants: primary: redwood-curie-v2-quant (int8/q4) on Dedicated; fallback variant: redwood-curie-v2-fp16 hosted. Also keep a lightweight distilled reranker (rerank-lite-v1) for cost-sensitive flows.
- Region routing: primary region us-west (proximity to their infra), regional fallback eu-west; if regional capacity fails, automatic fallback to Hosted API global endpoints.
- Rollback runbook: detect p95 spike >150% baseline OR error-rate >1% for 5m -> immediate traffic cutback to Hosted (graceful drain), alert SE + AE, run postmortem.
- Metrics to collect: p50/p95/p99 latency, cost per 1k tokens, error rate, model quality delta (BLEU/embedding-sim), KV cache hit rate.
Acceptance criteria (POC): p95 <= 250ms for 95% of traffic, end-to-end cost per 1M tokens reduced by >=20% vs Hosted baseline OR parity with improved tail latency, no data residency violations, SSO + audit logs verified.
Hardware preference noted: A100-class preferred for quantized models; SE to validate on available pool.
Security/compliance: legal asked for SOC2 artifacts + KMS integration guide (drive:/security/sg_oakridge_sso_faq.md).
Pricing: want committed monthly capacity quote; exploring 12-month vs 36-month reserved tier.
Quotes from call: "Need predictable tail latency — can't have >500ms spikes during business hours"; "Cost-per-conversation is our single biggest lever."
Open items: detailed canary config (thresholds), cost model calc for mixed traffic, confirm SLA for region failover, security evidence pack.
Short-term next steps: SE (Maya) to deliver canary config + synthetic load test plan by 2026-03-05; AE (Ravi) to circulate both pricing options; Legal to kick off SOC2 evidence exchange.
Longer-term: potential Dedicated contract + Optional Private deploy if residency demands increase.

title: Quantico Health
preview:
Account summary (healthcare / potential PHI):
- Evaluating Redwood for clinical summarization + patient inbox triage (internal clinician tooling; not patient-facing initially).
- Current state: quick prototype built on Hosted API; target state: Redwood Private (VPC) if evaluation passes.

Firmographics:
- HQ: US (NA). Team describes themselves as "mid-size care ops platform"; engineering team ~25.
- Stack: AWS primary; strict controls around PHI movement.

Use case + workload notes:
- Primary flows:
  - Summarize long clinical notes (2k-8k tokens input typical; sometimes 12k+), output 200-800 tokens.
  - Classify/triage patient messages (shorter; wants low latency).
  - Internal search over policy + care guidelines (embeddings + rerank later; not in first phase).
- Latency goals (stated on call):
  - TTFB/TTFT: < 1.5s for short triage prompts; < 3s acceptable for long summaries.
  - Throughput: initial low (dev + small pilot), but wants headroom for 50-100 concurrent users by end of Q2.
- Model posture: open to Redwood-recommended defaults; cares more about reliability + auditability than specific model brand.

Data handling / compliance requirements (recurring theme):
- Data residency:
  - US-only processing and storage. Explicit ask: "No silent cross-region failover to EU/APAC".
  - Wants region pinning documented + visible in contract / order form.
- Retention:
  - Preference is 0 retention for prompts/outputs (or customer-controlled retention window).
  - Operational logs: wants <= 30 days and ability to export audit logs to their SIEM.
  - Deletion: needs documented delete workflow and confirmation ("prove it was deleted").
- Access controls:
  - SSO/SAML required before pilot expansion.
  - Audit logging required (admin + API key creation + model/config changes).
  - KMS: asked whether Redwood Private supports customer-managed keys.
- HIPAA/BAA:
  - Asked directly if Redwood signs BAA. We did not commit; aligned that we can provide security posture + roadmap and route through legal.

Onboarding + activation friction (important context for this record):
- Early activation took longer than expected (2 days from signup to first successful request).
- Issues:
  1) Onboarding emails not received by 2 developers on the team (likely spam/quarantine). They referenced they "never got the API key email" and assumed setup was blocked.
     - Support linked to SUP-24871; Ben Carter looped in for deliverability investigation context.
  2) Console nudge bug interfered with API key creation for one user (nudge overlay/state issue on the key creation page).
     - Tracked as SUP-24905; workaround provided; later confirmed fixed.
  3) Confusion about what counts as "activation" vs "first request" in their internal checklist; they want a single canonical quickstart path.
- Outcome:
  - After support + workaround, they ran the minimal “hello world” snippet and achieved first request + streaming test.

Sales/marketing suppression notes:
- Do NOT include in paid retargeting or generic lifecycle marketing beyond transactional ops messages.
- Reason: healthcare + potential PHI + strict compliance posture; they explicitly requested minimal marketing touchpoints during evaluation.

Recent activity timeline:
- 2026-01-17: Inbound signup; notes mention "clinical summarization pilot".
- 2026-01-22: Initial discovery (Jordan Blake + Paula Mendes). Key asks: US-only, retention controls, audit logs.
- 2026-01-25: Support escalation: missing onboarding emails (SUP-24871). Guidance provided: whitelist sending domain + check spam quarantine.
- 2026-01-29: Support escalation: console nudge blocking API key creation (SUP-24905). Repro captured; fix confirmed in subsequent console update.
- 2026-02-04: Eval kickoff (technical) – reviewed Hosted API flow; discussed VPC architecture as likely next step.
- 2026-02-12: Sent security FAQ + initial answers on residency/retention; customer asked for "something we can forward to compliance".
- 2026-02-18: Security review session (audit logs, key mgmt, retention). Customer asked about deletion attestations + log export.
- 2026-02-26: Follow-up email from Quantico: wants a concrete proposal for Private (VPC) pilot scope + timeline.

Open questions / to confirm:
- Whether PHI will be included in the initial pilot (they said "de-identified at first" but not guaranteed long-term).
- Required US region (us-east-1 only vs multiple US regions) and whether they can accept manual failover.
- Exact retention knobs needed: 0-day for prompts/outputs vs hashed storage for abuse detection.

Next steps (internal):
- Paula to draft a 1-pager: residency + retention options for Hosted vs Private (VPC), including explicit limitations.
- Jordan to align with Michael Grant / Olga Petrov on what we can commit to in writing re: retention + deletion.
- If we can meet baseline, propose 30-day Private (VPC) evaluation with SSO + audit log export as success criteria.

Next steps (customer-facing):
- Provide VPC deployment overview + checklist for security review.
- Offer a guided re-onboarding session (15 min) to rebuild confidence post-email/nudge issues and validate their TTFT targets.

title: Acme AI
preview:
Account summary
- Existing Hosted API customer (self-serve -> sales-assisted expansion in late 2025). Heavy chat + some embeddings for internal knowledge base.
- Cost sensitive; measures $/resolved ticket and strongly cares about predictable unit economics.

Pricing / packaging experiment exposure
- Flagged into 2026Q1 pricing experiment cohort (Variant B) for a subset of workspaces starting ~2026-01-22.
  - Experiment tag in telemetry: EXP_PP_2026Q1_VARIANT_B (per Product Ops).
  - Customer noticed plan messaging + overage behavior differences across workspaces; asked for a single consolidated statement for Finance.
- Cohort caveat: meter fixes overlapped with experiment window; any usage deltas need pre/post segmentation in readouts.

Recent activity timeline (most recent first)
- 2026-02-25: Renewal / expansion check-in. Acme asked for spend forecast with "billed tokens" separated from cache hits. Wants commitment options (Dedicated) if it reduces variance.
- 2026-02-12: Internal: Finance pulled Acme into discrepancy sample set for Feb preview run (see invoice-discrepancy-samples-feb-2026).
- 2026-02-06: Call: Pricing & usage review (Fireflies link). Key quote: "We’re not trying to nickel-and-dime—just need the invoice to reconcile to what our engineers see."
- 2026-02-04 to 2026-02-10: Slack triage thread on invoice discrepancy and suspected retry double-counting; Eng confirmed issue pattern matched streaming retry/fallback path; fix queued.

Billing questions / issues (open + resolved)
1) Invoice tokens did not match usage API export (SUP-28451)
- Symptom: Daily export tokens ~2–4% lower than invoice preview for certain days/models; variance clustered by region.
- Current hypothesis (as of Feb): missing/incorrect region dimension in usage events caused pricing attribution drift in internal aggregation vs customer export slice.
- Mitigation: send reconciled export keyed on ledger aggregation + corrected region dimension once backfill is complete; document that "usage API" is source-of-truth for customer reporting but invoice is computed from billing ledger.

2) Suspected double-charged retries / fallback (SUP-28463)
- Symptom: Customer saw spikes during incidents where Redwood performed server-side retry/fallback; they believed they were billed twice for partial streams.
- Eng notes: streaming retry/fallback path could emit duplicate usage events under partial failure. Fix landed in Feb rollout (see PR references in internal threads).
- Status: Pending Finance decision on whether Feb invoice needs a goodwill credit vs strict forward-only correction. Acme explicitly asked for a credit if we confirm duplicate billing.

3) Token counting fairness (streaming + tool calls)
- Customer compares Redwood billed tokens vs their local tokenizer and saw mismatches on tool-call heavy prompts.
- Requested: simple explanation in writing: billed_tokens vs raw tokens, tool-call attribution, and whether cache hits are billable.
- Action: share the updated usage FAQ excerpt + provide a one-page explanation customized for Acme’s integration.

Technical / product requirements surfaced
- Usage reporting: needs daily usage export aligned to America/Los_Angeles business day; wants separate columns for prompt/completion/billed_tokens and cache_hit attribution.
- Idempotency: they will add idempotency keys client-side for retry safety; asked whether Redwood dedupes by request_id.
- Performance: prefers stable latency over absolute lowest cost for their production route; open to Dedicated if it offers steadier throughput.

Stakeholders (customer)
- Primary: Director of Engineering (platform), FinOps lead, and Procurement counsel.
- Champion: Platform Eng manager running the LLM gateway.

Internal owners / coordination
- AE/Owner: Jordan Blake (pulled in due to renewal + dispute sensitivity).
- SE: Monica Patel (SDK + retries/idempotency alignment).
- Finance: Emily Stone coordinating RevRec-safe remediation path; Rishi Malhotra watching margin impact.
- Eng: Logan Wright / Nadia Rahman for meter attribution; Connor O’Brien for shadow-billing rollout coordination.

Next steps (2 weeks)
- Provide Acme a "Feb usage reconciliation pack":
  - ledger totals vs usage-event aggregates vs customer usage export
  - explanation of billed_tokens vs raw tokens; include streaming + tool-call notes
  - call out known incident window(s) and whether they affected billing
- Confirm whether we will issue a credit for any verified duplicate metering (requires Finance + Legal sign-off).
- Renewal order form: incorporate updated usage definition language (retries/fallback, caching attribution, dispute window). Schedule procurement review once language is ready.

Links / references (internal)
- Slack: finance/1770043123-usage-recon-discrepancy-acme
- Fireflies: sales-calls/2026-02-06-pricing-and-usage-review-with-acme-ai
- Gmail: laura_bennett/20260205-metering-discrepancy-and-revrec-impact
- Jira: SUP-28451, SUP-28463

title: Calypso ServeFlow
preview:
Summary: Calypso ServeFlow is a fintech payments orchestration platform that wants to embed real-time conversational and recommendation features into their merchant dashboard. Very latency- and cost-sensitive — they price UX per-transaction and need predictable unit economics.

Key constraints / asks (from CTO + infra lead):
- SLO targets: median latency <= 35-50ms for 128-256 token chat paths; p95 < 75-90ms.
- Throughput: baseline 200 qps with bursts to 1k qps during peak settlement windows.
- Cost sensitivity: target incremental cost <= $0.50 per 1000 messages (after caching/batching).
- Model preferences: open Llama-style models for deterministic licensing; willing to evaluate small proprietary variants if latency improves.
- Routing/fallback: need policy for model fallback to lower-cost quantized variants during capacity pressure.
- Security: VPC + customer-managed KMS, audit logs retained 1 year, SSO/SAML for internal users, and EU data residency for some merchant data.

Optimization interest: batching (tunable max batch latency tradeoffs), KV/prefix caching for repeated prompts (merchant-specific templates), and FP16/8-bit quantization profiles. CTO asked: "Can you get similar quality at 4-bit quantized + dynamic dequant on GPU?" SE notes: test quantized 4/8-bit + Redwood Optimize recommendations.

Perf bench ask (explicit): produce benchmark matrix that varies: model variant (float16, 8-bit, 4-bit), batch window (1ms, 5ms, 20ms), cache hit rate (0%, 30%, 60%), sequence lengths (avg 120, 256, 512). Measure median/p95/p99 latency, throughput, tokens/sec, and cost per 1M tokens. Provide kernel/profile traces for hotspots.

POC results so far (notes):
- Baseline hosted API (float16, no cache): median ~62ms, p95 ~110ms at 200 qps.
- Experimental Dedicated with batching=5ms and KV cache (~40% hit): median ~38ms, p95 ~70ms, cost down ~37%.
- 4-bit quantization reduced GPU memory by ~2x; quality regression flagged on domain-specific prompts (fraud detection phrases).

Key constraints / asks (from CTO + infra lead):
- SLO targets: median latency <= 35-50ms for 128-256 token chat paths; p95 < 75-90ms.
- Throughput: baseline 200 qps with bursts to 1k qps during peak settlement windows.
- Cost sensitivity: target incremental cost <= $0.50 per 1000 messages (after caching/batching).
- Model preferences: open Llama-style models for deterministic licensing; willing to evaluate small proprietary variants if latency improves.
- Routing/fallback: need policy for model fallback to lower-cost quantized variants during capacity pressure.
- Security: VPC + customer-managed KMS, audit logs retained 1 year, SSO/SAML for internal users, and EU data residency for some merchant data.

title: Cobalt Orbit Inference
preview:
Background:
- Mid-size logistics platform, operates real-time route optimization + driver assistant chat. High-read consistency for embeddings + sub-100ms soft SLO for short chat turns on edge routes.
- Interested in Redwood Dedicated for predictable throughput and data isolation; on-prem option for EU-sensitive customers.

Primary objectives (from AE/SE discussions):
- Throughput guarantees for business hours regional peaks: target 12k RPS of small-chat tokens across 3 regions (NA/EU/APAC) with 99th percentile latency SLOs: 50-120ms depending on model variant.
- Predictable reserved GPU planning: want recommendation for reserved vs burst mix (monthly commit + burst on demand). Need example ARRs for 4x/6x utilization scenarios.
- Reliability: cross-region failover with <5s cold-swap for routing + model-variant fallback when primary variants saturate. Documented sequence for model fallbacks and token-level impact.
- SLA negotiation points: explicit MTTI for failover, credit matrix for SLO breaches, and runbook sharing for DR tests.

Technical constraints / preferences:
- Favor open/quantized model variants for cost (int8/int4) but require quality gates.
- KV cache warmup time critical for long-session chat — want plan for pre-warmed caches across dedicated pools.
- Network egress restrictions for EU customers -> must support customer-managed VPC + private endpoints.
- Must support SAML SSO + audit log forwarding to SIEM.

POC design agreed (high level):
- Phase 1 (2 weeks): Benchmarks with synthetic replay of 72-hour peak pattern. Measure p50/p95/p99 per-model and token cost.
- Phase 2 (3 weeks): Failover drills across NA->EU and EU->APAC; validate routing TTLs, DNS vs anycast tradeoffs, and latency spike behavior.
- Phase 3 (2 weeks): Model-variant saturation tests: define 3 variants (high-accuracy, balanced, low-cost quantized) and exercise automatic fallback policy while recording quality metrics.

Key questions we raised for Redwood team:
- Can Dedicated expose metric streams for per-pool cache hit rates and KV cache warm-up? (SE: yes, via console + tracing hooks).
- How are reserved GPU SKUs billed when autoscaling triggers burst capacity? Need sample invoice.
- Can we get a templated SLA addendum that includes cross-region failover RTO/RPO and credits?

Quotes / paraphrases from customer calls:
- \"If failover takes longer than the billing cycle, we lose SLAs with our fleet customers.\"
- \"We need to know exact behavior when the primary model is saturated — do you queue, reject, or downgrade model?\"

Action items (AE/SE):
- Jordan: circulate Dedicated sizing spreadsheet with 3 plan options (conservative / baseline / aggressive) by 2026-02-24 (done).
- Priya (SE): provide synthetic load scripts and initial benchmark harness; propose cache warm-up pattern by 2026-02-28.
- Marcus (CSM): draft SLA addendum template + examples of credits for p99 breaches by 2026-03-03.

Open risks / notes to legal/procurement:
- Customer will push for a shorter penalty window for failover SLA (30m) vs Redwood standard (2h). Expect negotiation.
- Data residency: some fleets require EU-only model inference. Need clear routing controls and regional isolation docs.

Stubs / references:
- POC benchmark plan: drive.google.com/open?id=cobalt-poc-bench-plan
- Security FAQ reviewed: drive.google.com/open?id=cobalt-security-faq
- Transcript: ff-2026-02-14-7892 (contains CSO Q&A on KMS + key rotation)

Short next-step summary:
- Redwood to deliver dedicated SKU proposal + failover runbook draft. Legal to exchange SLA addendum. POC kickoff when SOW signed.

title: Nestwell Inbound AI
preview:
Inbound SMB lead via self-serve signup. Started trial 2026-02-10, small volume (a few thousand tokens/day) but high sensitivity around customer PII handling. Primary contact: Mara Lin (Head of Product). Key asks: 1) SOC2 Type 2 evidence summary (not full report) 2) Sub-processors list (who processes logs, metrics, model infra) 3) DPA with standard EU clauses even though HQ in NA — wants DPA to be ready for legal review. They use AWS us-east-1; prefer public cloud hosted API. Cost sensitivity: high, want to keep monthly spend < $2k while scaling tests. "Mara: we love the latency numbers but need paperwork before ramp".
- Trial usage shows chat + doc-search combo, mainly conversational support autopilot and embedding-based KB.
- Security/Legal pinged us 2026-02-16 with a 12-question mini-questionnaire (attached). We answered 6/12, asked for SOC2 summary and subprocessors list for remaining.
- Requested retention policy options (default 30 days vs. 0 retention for responses) — product asked if we can surface short-term retention flag on API keys.
- Asked about KMS support; marked as optional for now, may require in future.
- No dedicated infra requirements at this stage.
- Quote request for projected 3mo POC (est. $1-4k/month).

title: HatchLab Copilot
preview:
Summary: HatchLab builds an IDE copilot focused on test-run orchestration and live build logs. They want function-calling so the assistant can invoke test runners, linters, and a sandboxed build agent from inside the IDE and stream logs back in <200ms for interactive UX.

Key requirements / constraints:
- Sandboxed function-calls: must run in hermetic containers with no outbound access, ephemeral FS, and resource limits. Customer insists on 'no internet egress' from build agents.
- Low-latency streaming: in-IDE tail of build logs must be near-real-time (target <200ms tail-to-client for small builds; acceptable up to 500ms for larger CI jobs).
- Model/version pinning: want deterministic assistants tied to specific model versions (pinning for reproducible behavior during test runs); ability to rollback to older quantized model if regression detected.
- Tooling surface: function call APIs need a constrained capability set (run_tests, run_lint, stream_build_logs, fetch_artifact_meta) with RBAC.
- Audit & retention: all function-call invocations and full logs must be retained with audit entries; legal asks for 3+ years of retention metadata.
- KMS + signing: artifacts produced by the POC agent must be signed with customer KMS key or HSM-backed key management.

Customer quotes / color: "If the copilot runs tests for me, we need guarantees that it can't exfiltrate secrets or reach the public internet." — Lena Park, CTO.

Technical notes from SE call: we've scoped two approaches for the POC: 1) Private deployment in customer's VPC with subnet-level ACLs and egress blocker, 2) On-prem partner-supplied appliance for fully air-gapped runs. Customer prefers #1 to start.

Value drivers: reduce developer context switching (in-IDE test feedback), faster triage (live logs + reranker for stack traces), and lower infra cost by batching test-run requests and reusing cached build artifacts.

Concise ToDos: finalize network peering window with infra team, deliver VPC template, send updated pricing for Dedicated vs Private, confirm legal SLA language around retained logs.

title: GrainWave Commerce
preview:
- SMB ecommerce platform building AI-powered product search and conversational support.
- Primary objective: replace current embed/rerank pipeline (Algolia + custom ranking) to reduce infra ops.
- Volume profile: P0: chat-based support (bursty, low latency), P1: embeddings for search (batch hourly), estimated ~150-250k tokens/month initially.
- Cost sensitivity: moderate — early stage, expects self-serve pricing to be competitive; will expand if unit economics hold.
- Security ask: 'Can you provide SOC2 Type II and list of sub-processors? Our GC needs DPA before billing/account upgrade.' — paraphrase from Rajesh.
- Data residency: majority NA, but ~8% EU customers; legal wants confirmation on whether EU data can be pinned to eu-1 region or if public-cloud multi-region is used.
- Preferred models: open-source fine if hosted by Redwood; want a small-latency model for chat (<=200ms p95) and embedding model comparable to OpenAI text-embedding-3-small.
- Routing/fallback: prefer automatic fallback to lower-cost model when latency SLOs are met, must preserve deterministic safety filters.
- Notes: they use Okta SSO (SAML) and require audit logs for API keys.
- Quote from Rajesh: 'We need the DPA and a simple sub-processor list — legal is the pace limiter here.'
- Internal: good fit for Hosted API self-serve path; low friction if SOC2 artifacts and DPA are provided quickly.
- Recommended pack: send hosted quickstart + SOC2 FAQ + standard DPA template; attach sub-processors CSV and point to console audit logs doc.

title: Aquifer Autonomous Insights
preview:
Lead: Jordan Blake (AE). SE: Priya Menon. CSM: Alex Ruiz assigned for transition planning.

Discovery summary:
- 2025-10-07: Intro call; Aquifer uses LLMs for vehicle incident triage + operator chat. Interested in dedicated GPU pools for deterministic latency.
- 2025-11-12: Shared annotated prompt set (2.1k examples) and initial traffic profile (peak 600 rps for embeddings; 120 rps chat during surge).
- 2025-12-02: Security questionnaire submitted; SOC2 + KMS required. Data residency: US only for PII subsets.
- 2026-01-18: Architecture workshop with SE team; discussed KV-cache, batching, and multi-model routing for cost control.
- 2026-02-10: POC kickoff call (Fireflies id ff_20260210_aurora-poc-call). Agreed test plan and metrics.

POC goals (what Aquifer needs):
- Validate sustained throughput on Dedicated pool: 3k tokens/sec sustained with 99% of requests meeting latency SLO.
- Tail latency SLOs: p95 < 120ms for 64-token responses; p99 < 350ms for 128-token responses.
- Cost profile: unit token cost target to be 30-40% below current cloud-hosted baseline using batching + prefix cache.
- Reliability: 99.9% uptime SLA, cross-region fallback during planned maintenance.

POC test plan (high level):
- Load tests: synthetic traffic generated from k6 sim with replayed request shapes (chat short, chat long, embeddings, rerank). Target ramps to peak load observed in prod profile.
- Soak tests: 72-hour run at 70% of peak reserved capacity to surface memory leaks, KV cache saturation, and autoscale behavior.
- Representative prompts: 2.1k annotated prompts plus 1k support transcripts for safety/regression checks.
- Metrics captured: p50/p95/p99 latency, tokens/sec, GPU utilization per instance, batch size distribution, error/retry rate, cost per 1M tokens.
- Quality gates: BLEU/similarity and a small eval harness to detect regressions vs baseline open model.

Technical/Capacity notes:
- Aquifer expects to reserve capacity equivalent to 8xA100-80GB (or equivalent q4/8 quantized V100 class) for initial staging; will iterate after POC.
- Prefers model routing policies: primary high-accuracy variant during business hours, budget variant for batch jobs.
- KMS integration required before production cutover; security team flagged HSM requirement for keys used to unwrap telemetry.

Negotiation/Commercial items in play:
- SLA: Aquifer pushing for 99.95% with financial credits; Redwood proposed 99.9% for Dedicated baseline and escalation path.
- Pricing: committed capacity discount tiers; Aquifer requests committed-month-to-month flexibility for first 3 months of POC-to-prod ramp.
- Term: procurement evaluating 12–24 month reservation options.

Recent activity and decisions:
- 2026-02-12: Runbook and JMeter scripts received from Aquifer infra. SE finalized native k6 test harness.
- 2026-02-14: Security sent SOC2 report; awaiting KMS attestation.
- 2026-02-15: Dry run on a small Dedicated staging pool to validate tooling and metrics capture. p95 for 64-token responses ~110ms, p99 ~320ms — promising.

Open items / Blockers (context):
- Legal redlines: Aquifer legal added HSM + data residency clauses that require review (ticket SEC-4104).
- On-prem option: Aquifer evaluating air-gapped subset; procurement timeline for on-prem infra is 8-12 weeks which would push production start.
- KMS path: need confirmation whether customer will use cloud KMS or bring their own HSM; affects rollout schedule.

Next steps (operational):
- Week of 2026-02-20: Start 72-hour soak at agreed 70% capacity on Dedicated staging pool. SE to monitor and feed real-time dashboards to Aquifer SREs.
- 2026-03-04: Review soak results, adjust reserved GPU sizing and batching configs.
- 2026-03-10: Legal and Redwood finalize SLA redlines; commercial to issue committed capacity quote for 12 months.

Sales notes (short):
- High strategic: potential 500k-1M ARR first year if SLA + KMS cleared.
- Sensitive to unit cost; Optimize team to run quantization and batching recommendations during POC.
- Tone: pragmatic, engineering-led buying group.

Quoted lines: "We need predictable tail latency during surge windows — interruptions equal customer downtime for operators," - head of platform (paraphrase).

title: Acme Health
preview:
Primary workload: (1) clinician-facing summarization of long-form notes (streaming preferred), (2) internal doc-search/RAG over policies/procedures, (3) support-agent style Q&A for staff. Throughput: initial ~50–150 concurrent users, spiky during business hours; latency goal stated as “<2s time-to-first-token for typical prompts” for the summarization UI. Strong cost sensitivity for long-context summaries; asked about batching/caching and model/quantization options.

Deployment preference: Private (VPC) strongly preferred; Dedicated is a fallback only if we can document strict isolation, retention controls, and clear shared responsibility. They explicitly do not want prompts/responses retained beyond what is necessary for operations.

Security/compliance: SOC 2 Type II report required under NDA. Asked multiple times about HIPAA/BAA: want a vendor willing to sign a BAA for any workflow that could include PHI. SSO is required (Okta); SCIM requested for user lifecycle. RBAC: need separation between platform admins (security team), billing admins, and app developers. Audit logging: must cover admin actions + auth events; export to SIEM. Encryption: in transit TLS; at rest encryption with preference for customer-managed keys (KMS) in Private. Retention/deletion: need clear default retention statement and ability to configure log retention; also asked for deletion SLA for transient request data.

title: Ironbrook DataWorks
preview:
Summary: Mid-market B2B product analytics company. Started with Redwood Hosted API during Dec trial to validate embeddings + chat for their in-app support assistant and a reranking pipeline for search relevance. Trial showed good quality but cost and latency variability during spikes triggered evaluation for Dedicated/reserved capacity.

Trial metrics observed (hosted):
- average QPS during weekday: 4 qps (observed 2026-01-04 to 2026-01-31)
- peak QPS observed during product release: 12 qps (single 3-hour burst)
- observed typical concurrency: 6 concurrent streams, peaks to 28 concurrent streams during load tests
- token mix: prompts ~350 tokens on average, completions ~150 tokens average; embeddings calls ~512 vector dims, 45% of calls are embeddings, 35% chat/generation, 20% reranking/short prompts
- P50 latency (hosted): 90ms for embeddings, 220ms for chat when KV cache hit; P95 spike to 800ms during bursts

Capacity ask (from customer forecasting exercise):
- target steady-state QPS: 20 qps (expected after 3-month ramp)
- target peak QPS: 120 qps (marketing/feature releases, 1-2x per month spikes up to 2-3 hours)
- target steady concurrency for chat: 40 concurrent streams, peak concurrency 240
- token throughput target: ~14k tokens/sec steady, burst up to ~85k tokens/sec (weighted by completion sizes)
- latency SLO: chat <= 200ms P50, <= 400ms P95 for primary region (na). Reclustering reranker acceptable up to 600ms for background jobs.

Requested Dedicated sizing options discussed:
- Option A (balanced): 2 reserved GPU pools, each 8 x T4-equivalent (inferred), with autoscale headroom to 12 GPUs for burst windows; expected to cover steady 20 qps and moderate bursts.
- Option B (performance): 4 x A10p-equivalent nodes in dedicated pool + low-latency NVLink config for KV cache sharing. Shown as preferred if we need sub-200ms P50 at 120 qps bursts.
- Option C (cost-first): mixed-quantized model variants + aggressive caching, 1 reserved pool + on-demand burst, lower SLA.

Cost sensitivity: moderate-high. Finance wants 12-month committed pricing with clear unit cost per 1k tokens and defined overage bands. Procurement flagged requirement: ability to cap monthly spend and automatic failover to cheaper variant if over threshold.

Security/compliance notes:
- must support SSO (SAML), SCIM provisioning for dev orgs.
- require SOC2 + audit logging retention 1 year min.
- EU customer subset -> need data residency in eu-west-1 for user PII used in reranking training set.

POC milestones and acceptance criteria (customer):
- 1) Integration with hosted API complete + smoke tests (done)
- 2) End-to-end reranker POC using Redwood embeddings and rerank API: precision lift >= 8% over current baseline (in progress)
- 3) Performance test on Dedicated config: achieve <=200ms P50 at 20 qps and <=400ms P95 at 60 qps for chat (requires dry-run on Dedicated staging)
- 4) Cost report generated using Redwood Optimize with token-level breakdown and recommended quantization profile (deliverable requested)

Quotes from calls: "Quality is there; what worries us is the cost variability when we scale and the latency spikes during marketing bursts. If we can get predictable pricing + dedicated pool sizing, we can move forward."

Key internal champions: Head of Platform (Eva Morales) - product sponsor; VP Ops (Samir Rao) - procurement gate; ML Lead (Priya Nair) - technical POC owner.

Notes on integration: backend uses Node.js SDK (server-side), routing layer in GCP us-central1. They want Redwood Dedicated in the same region with optional cross-region fallback to eu-west-1 for EU customers.

Recommended immediate actions for AE/SE:
- deliver Dedicated sizing worksheet (completed draft 2026-02-06) with three scenarios and cost bands
- schedule technical kickoff for Dedicated staging test week of 2026-02-22
- provide draft SLA language and residency proof for EU to procurement by 2026-02-14

Proposed timeline: Decision expected by 2026-06. Procurement needs final pricing + contract within 3 weeks after performance test success.

title: Devflow AI
preview:
HubSpot Company Record — Devflow AI (devflow.ai)

Summary
- Mid-market devtools team building an LLM-powered coding assistant inside their IDE + web app.
- Came in via self-serve Hosted API; now in evaluation with potential Dedicated capacity request if p95 latency holds at launch traffic.
- Strong sensitivity to onboarding friction (they’re modeling their own user onboarding after ours).

Lifecycle touchpoints (sprint instrumentation)
- Onboarding Email v2: received (Variant B; subject emphasized “fast first request”).
  - Observed behavior: clicked CTA → quickstart page → returned to console within ~6 minutes.
  - Feedback: “The email got us to the right page, but the snippet didn’t match our setup (we’re TS/Node + Vite).”
- Console nudges:
  - Nudge shown: Create API key (completed).
  - Nudge shown: Make your first request (clicked; led to docs). Dismissal persisted correctly after completion (no repeat complaint).
  - Nudge shown: Enable streaming (they asked what it changes for TTFT; wants best-practice defaults).

Activation / product usage notes
- Activation milestones (qualitative from call + event notes):
  - First successful request: achieved after initial 401 error (likely copied wrong env var / key mismatch).
  - Time-to-first-token: “Felt fine locally” but wants measured p95 under concurrency.
  - First 100k tokens: not yet at time of call; they were still prototyping prompt formats.
- SDK notes:
  - Using TypeScript SDK.
  - Early error handling: they hit “missing/invalid API key” path; asked for clearer guidance in-console on where the key is expected (env var name + example).

Evaluation requirements (from 2026-01-22 call)
- Workload
  - Interactive chat/code completion; many short prompts, frequent streaming.
  - Potential embeddings for repo/doc indexing (not immediate).
- Performance targets
  - Wants “snappy” streaming start; specifically asked to minimize perceived delay at response start.
  - Interested in batching/caching guidance later (Optimize mentioned as follow-on).
- Reliability / ops
  - Wants predictable behavior during capacity contention; asked about fallback behavior and how errors present to the client.
- Security/compliance
  - SOC 2 requested.
  - Asked about audit logs and retention controls (they sometimes pass code snippets from customers).
  - SSO/SAML: “nice to have” now, may become required if they roll to larger enterprise accounts.

Activation blockers / friction points
- 401 on first request (copy/paste / env var mismatch). They described it as: “We weren’t sure if it was the key, the model name, or our network.”
  - Suggested mitigation: tighten first-request snippet + highlight the exact env var name; in-console status check for ‘key seen’ would help.
- Docs mismatch for their stack (TS/Node). They want a minimal snippet that matches the SDK they installed.
- Confusion around streaming defaults:
  - They asked whether they should always stream, and what settings are recommended to optimize TTFT vs throughput.
- Email deliverability: no issue reported by Devflow AI directly, but note: their domain has strict filtering; keep an eye if future lifecycle sends bounce/complain.

Stakeholder notes
- Champion: Head of Product Engineering (name redacted in CRM export); very hands-on.
- Technical evaluator: platform engineer who owns their inference layer; wants a short ‘load-test recipe’.

Internal follow-ups / links
- Fireflies transcript: fireflies/sales-calls/2026-01-22-onboarding-feedback-devflow-ai
- Related lifecycle experiment context: lifecycle growth sprint launch brief (ben_carter/20260127-launch-brief-lifecycle-growth-experiments)
- Messaging consistency thread used for copy alignment: soojin_lee/20260118-messaging-review-onboarding-sequence
- Support reference (not necessarily this account, but relevant symptom pattern): SUP-24871 ‘not receiving onboarding emails’

Next steps
- Request a sample prompt distribution + expected concurrency to run a lightweight benchmark (include streaming on/off comparison).
- Send SOC 2 + retention controls summary; confirm if they need SSO/SAML before launch.
- If performance targets are strict, introduce Dedicated option with a small reserved pool for launch month.

Latest AE/SE note (2026-02-07)
- They’re leaning positive on Hosted API for pilot, but want a clear plan for scaling (Dedicated) and guardrails (fallbacks, quotas) before committing to a launch timeline.
